questions.scenario: A proposed rule says `Allow Any Any to Any`. What is the main review problem?

options.text: It does not name the expected source, destination, protocol, or port, so the blast radius is unclear.
It uses words instead of Azure resource IDs.
It will never match any traffic.
It proves the app code needs a retry loop.

questions.explanation: A broad rule may work, but it hides intent and expands exposure. Review should require a named source, destination, protocol, and port.

questions.scenario: Why are ASGs useful in a beginner-friendly rule?

options.text: They let the rule describe app roles such as `asg-app-gateway` and `asg-orders-api` instead of raw IP lists.

questions.explanation: Application security groups make rules read closer to application roles. They do not replace routing, certificates, or RBAC.

questions.scenario: What should a release record include for an NSG change?

options.text: The old and new rule intent.
The expected source, destination, protocol, and port.
Only the portal blade where the change was made.

questions.explanation: A release record should help another person verify or roll back the change. A portal location is not enough.
